query:
UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Hard hats are required on all construction sites where there is a risk of head injury from falling objects, bumping into fixed objects, or accidental contact with electrical hazards (OSHA 29 CFR 1926.100).
• There are two types: Type I protects the top of the head only; Type II protects the top and sides. For framing and steel work, Type II is recommended due to lateral impact risks.
• Inspect your hard hat daily: look for cracks, dents, or UV damage (chalky/faded appearance). The suspension system should be firm with no frayed straps. Replace the shell every 5 years and the suspension every 12 months, or sooner if damaged.
• Never drill holes in a hard hat, paint it with non-approved paint, or store it in direct sunlight (UV degrades the plastic). Don''t wear it backwards unless it''s specifically designed and rated for reverse wear.
• Hard hats must fit snugly with the suspension adjusted to your head size. A loose hard hat can fall off at the worst possible moment. The brim should be level, not tilted back.
• In cold weather, use a winter liner designed for hard hats — don''t substitute a beanie that pushes the hat up off your head.
• Real-world example: A steel connector was hit by a falling bolt from two stories up. His hard hat cracked but absorbed the impact — he walked away with a headache instead of a skull fracture. The hard hat was only 6 months old.

Discussion Questions:
1. When did you last inspect your hard hat''s suspension?
2. Is anyone wearing a hard hat that''s more than 5 years old?
3. Are we using the right type (I or II) for the hazards on this job?

Sign-off: Your hard hat is your first line of defense. Treat it like your life depends on it — because it does.' WHERE `id` = 7;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Safety glasses with side shields are the minimum requirement on construction sites. They must meet ANSI Z87.1 standards — look for the Z87 marking on the lens or frame.
• Match your eye protection to the hazard: safety glasses for impact (nailing, cutting), goggles for dust and chemical splash, face shields for grinding and cutting operations, and welding helmets with proper shade for welding.
• Prescription glasses are NOT safety glasses unless they have Z87-rated lenses and side shields. If you wear prescription glasses, get safety glasses that fit over them or invest in prescription safety glasses.
• Clean your safety glasses daily. Scratched lenses reduce visibility and can cause eye strain. Replace them when scratches impair your vision.
• Contact lenses can be worn with safety glasses, but they do NOT replace eye protection. Dust and debris can get trapped under contacts, causing severe irritation.
• If something gets in your eye, do NOT rub it. Use an eyewash station or clean water to flush for at least 15 minutes. Know where the nearest eyewash station is on your job site.
• Real-world example: A carpenter was cutting treated lumber without safety glasses. A wood chip hit his eye and embedded in the cornea. He needed emergency surgery and was off work for 3 weeks. Safety glasses cost $5 — the surgery cost $12,000.

Discussion Questions:
1. Where is the nearest eyewash station on this job site?
2. Is everyone wearing the right type of eye protection for their current task?
3. When was the last time you replaced your scratched safety glasses?

Sign-off: You only get two eyes. Protect them every single time.' WHERE `id` = 8;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Your hands are your most important tools — hand injuries account for nearly 25% of all construction injuries. The right gloves can prevent cuts, punctures, burns, and chemical exposure.
• Match gloves to the task: Cut-resistant gloves (ANSI A4 or higher) for handling sheet metal, steel, and sharp materials. Leather gloves for general construction, handling lumber, and rough materials. Chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile or neoprene) for solvents, adhesives, and coatings. Welding gloves for hot work.
• Check the ANSI cut level rating: A1-A3 for light duty, A4-A6 for medium (framing, steel handling), A7-A9 for heavy duty (glass, sharp metal). For framing work, A4 minimum is recommended.
• Inspect gloves before each use: look for holes, tears, thin spots, and degraded material. Wet gloves lose grip — keep a dry pair available.
• Gloves should fit snugly but not restrict movement. Loose gloves can get caught in machinery. Too-tight gloves reduce dexterity and cause fatigue.
• Remove rings and jewelry before putting on gloves. A ring can catch on equipment and cause a degloving injury — one of the most severe hand injuries in construction.
• Real-world example: A laborer was handling steel studs without cut-resistant gloves. A sharp edge sliced through his work gloves and cut three tendons in his hand. He was out for 4 months and needed two surgeries. Cut-resistant gloves would have prevented the injury entirely.

Discussion Questions:
1. What type of gloves should we be using for today''s tasks?
2. Does everyone have gloves that fit properly?
3. Is anyone wearing rings or jewelry that should be removed?

Sign-off: The right gloves for the right job. No exceptions.' WHERE `id` = 9;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• OSHA requires hearing protection when noise exposure exceeds 85 decibels (dB) over an 8-hour shift (29 CFR 1926.52). Common construction tools that exceed this: circular saws (100 dB), nail guns (97 dB), hammer drills (114 dB), and generators (96 dB).
• Noise-induced hearing loss is permanent and irreversible. It happens gradually — by the time you notice, significant damage is already done. Protect your hearing now.
• Two main types: Earplugs (foam or pre-molded) reduce noise by 25-33 dB. Earmuffs reduce by 20-30 dB. For very loud environments (jackhammering, concrete cutting), use both together for maximum protection.
• Foam earplugs must be inserted correctly: roll the plug into a tight cylinder, pull your ear up and back to straighten the ear canal, insert the plug, and hold it for 30 seconds while it expands. A poorly inserted earplug provides almost no protection.
• Replace disposable foam earplugs daily. Reusable plugs should be cleaned regularly and replaced when they lose their shape or elasticity.
• If you experience ringing in your ears (tinnitus) after work, your hearing protection is inadequate. Talk to your supervisor about upgrading your protection.
• Real-world example: A 35-year-old framer who never wore hearing protection was diagnosed with moderate hearing loss in both ears — equivalent to a 60-year-old''s hearing. He now wears hearing aids. His coworker who always wore earplugs has normal hearing.

Discussion Questions:
1. What are the loudest tools and activities on this job site?
2. Does everyone know how to properly insert foam earplugs?
3. Has anyone experienced ringing in their ears after work?

Sign-off: You can''t get your hearing back. Wear protection every time the tools come out.' WHERE `id` = 10;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Heat illness kills construction workers every year, especially in Utah''s summer months when temperatures regularly exceed 100F. It progresses from heat cramps to heat exhaustion to heat stroke — heat stroke is a medical emergency that can be fatal.
• Water, Rest, Shade — the three pillars of heat illness prevention. Drink water every 15-20 minutes even if you''re not thirsty. By the time you feel thirsty, you''re already dehydrated.
• Know the warning signs: Heat cramps (muscle cramps, heavy sweating). Heat exhaustion (heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness, headache, cool/clammy skin). Heat stroke (hot/dry skin, confusion, loss of consciousness, body temp above 103F) — call 911 immediately.
• Acclimatization is critical. New workers and those returning from time off need 7-14 days to adjust to heat. During this period, gradually increase workload and take more frequent breaks.
• Schedule the hardest physical work for early morning or late afternoon. Rotate workers between sun-exposed and shaded tasks. Set up shade structures and cooling stations.
• Avoid caffeine and energy drinks — they increase dehydration. Sports drinks can help replace electrolytes but water should be the primary fluid.
• Real-world example: A laborer in St. George, UT collapsed on a 108F day. He had only been on the job for 3 days and wasn''t acclimatized. He hadn''t taken a water break in over an hour. He was treated for heat stroke and hospitalized for 2 days. The crew now has mandatory 15-minute water breaks every hour when temps exceed 90F.

Discussion Questions:
1. Where are our water and shade stations set up today?
2. Is anyone new to the job or returning from time off who needs acclimatization?
3. What''s the plan if someone shows signs of heat exhaustion?

Sign-off: Watch out for each other. If a coworker looks off, get them to shade and water immediately.' WHERE `id` = 11;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Cold stress occurs when the body loses heat faster than it can produce it. In Utah''s winter months, wind chill can make effective temperatures dangerously low, especially at elevation on open framing or steel structures.
• Know the stages: Frostbite (numbness, white/gray skin, especially fingers, toes, ears, nose) and Hypothermia (shivering, confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness — this is a medical emergency, call 911).
• Dress in layers: Base layer (moisture-wicking), insulation layer (fleece or wool), outer layer (wind/waterproof). Avoid cotton — it holds moisture and accelerates heat loss. Keep extra dry clothes on site.
• Protect extremities: insulated gloves (that still allow grip for tools), warm socks (wool or synthetic), insulated boots, and a hard hat liner. 40% of body heat is lost through the head and neck.
• Take warm-up breaks every 30-60 minutes in heated areas. Drink warm fluids (not alcohol — it dilates blood vessels and increases heat loss). Eat high-calorie foods to fuel your body''s heat production.
• Metal tools and surfaces conduct cold rapidly. Use insulated grips and avoid prolonged bare-skin contact with metal in freezing temperatures — skin can freeze to metal surfaces instantly.
• Real-world example: A steel erector in Park City was working at 15F with wind chill of -5F. He didn''t take breaks and his fingers went numb. He didn''t notice frostbite developing until his gloves came off — he lost the tips of two fingers. Mandatory warm-up breaks could have prevented this.

Discussion Questions:
1. What''s the temperature and wind chill forecast for today?
2. Does everyone have proper layered clothing and dry backup clothes?
3. Where is our warm-up area, and how often should we rotate to it?

Sign-off: Cold kills quietly. Layer up, take breaks, and watch your crew.' WHERE `id` = 12;
